costs: One-off design and legal-drafting costs for minimum-viability thresholds and cost-visibility rules
One-off IT build and ongoing data-management costs for the closure/outcome registry, including data-protection compliance
Recurring costs for standardized teacher-training module rollout and staffing of analytic/monitoring functions
Ongoing funding-responsibility allocation across ministry, tankerület, and municipal levels, currently unspecified and requiring explicit division
content.equity_impact: As a procedural, enabling framework rather than a direct service-delivery model, S5's equity impact depends entirely on downstream use: if thresholds and the registry are designed from inception to require disaggregated reporting by settlement size, ethnicity, and income, and if cost-visibility rules explicitly surface transport and mitigation costs, the framework could materially improve equity oversight across S1-S4; but if it is implemented as an aggregate, ethnicity-blind system, it risks providing a veneer of evidence-based legitimacy to decisions that remain as inequitable as under the status quo, since transparency alone does not compel any particular equity-protective action.
expected_benefits: Provides a transparent, evidence-based reference point for local decisions currently made on a discretionary, case-by-case basis, addressing a documented gap in Hungarian education law.
Standardized teacher training for multigrade and small-school pedagogy could address the documented preparation gap seen in both Finnish and inferred Hungarian contexts.

A closure/outcome registry would, over time, enable retrospective evaluation of which models (S1-S4) perform best under which conditions, informing future policy—though this benefit accrues only after multi-year data accumulation and is not immediate.
content.goal: Establish a national framework of transparent minimum-viability thresholds, cost-visibility rules, teacher-training standardization, and a closure/outcome registry to inform (not dictate) local decisions across any of the above models.

implementation_steps.action: Design and publish transparent minimum-viability thresholds and cost-visibility rules, with realistic allowance for legal, data-protection, and stakeholder-consultation review before publication.
Build and populate a closure/outcome registry disaggregated by settlement size, ethnicity, and income, with data-protection and legal-basis clearance secured before data collection begins.
Roll out standardized multigrade and small-school pedagogy training modules into initial and in-service teacher education, sequenced with realistic curriculum-development and accreditation lead time.

mechanisms: No bright-line statutory minimum enrollment threshold currently exists in Hungarian education law, unlike comparative models such as Ukraine's 45-student rule; closure decisions operate on a discretionary, case-by-case basis, creating unpredictability that a transparent national threshold framework would address.

Given that the curated source registry available for this topic contains no equivalent Hungarian closure/outcome dataset and multiple experts independently note the absence of systematic tracking, the claim that 'no current national registry of closure decisions and outcomes exists in Hungary' is a reasonable inference from an absence of evidence rather than a directly documented negative finding.

Finnish rural village-school teachers report being unprepared for multigrade teaching during initial teacher education, despite multigrade instruction being their routine working environment, illustrating a training-standardization gap that a national framework's teacher-training standards component would target.

Historical Hungarian small-school subsidies were deemed 'economically fully justified' by a 2007 analysis, yet base normatívák often fell short of actual per-pupil costs, indicating that cost-visibility rules could correct a documented historical mismatch between subsidy design and real costs.
risks: A non-binding, advisory registry and threshold framework may be politically instrumentalized: government or tankerület actors could selectively cite thresholds to justify predetermined closure decisions while ignoring them when politically inconvenient, absent explicit rules on how thresholds may or may not be used in decisions.

Sustained multi-year funding for the registry, training rollout, and analytic staffing is vulnerable to budget-cycle discontinuity and administration change, given the documented absence of cross-party consensus mechanisms and the chronic under-budgeting pattern already observed in the tankerület/KLIK system.
content.title: National systemic framework: transparency, thresholds and evidence infrastructure
